overfire_comparison.reviewer_note: reviewer_v5 already 8/8 in STEP E; both roles ≥7/8 ⇔ security reaches ≥7/8 here

findings.title: SQL injection
findings.attack_vector: An attacker can inject SQL through the `id` parameter in the URL, for example, `GET /todos/1 OR 1=1` to return all rows.
findings.mitigation: Use parameterized queries with placeholders and bound parameters for SQL statements.

findings.title: SQL injection
findings.attack_vector: An attacker can inject SQL through the `title` parameter in the POST body, for example by sending a payload like "'); DROP TABLE todos;--".
findings.mitigation: Use parameterized queries with placeholders and bound parameters for SQL statements.

findings.title: Missing input validation
findings.attack_vector: Invalid or maliciously crafted input can be processed without checks, potentially leading to errors or misuse.
findings.mitigation: Implement input validation to check the type, length, and format of the `title` field.

findings.title: SQL injection
findings.attack_vector: req.params.id is concatenated directly into the SQL query string without sanitization, allowing SQL injection.
findings.mitigation: Use a parameterized query with placeholders and bound parameters for the id value.

findings.title: SQL injection
findings.attack_vector: req.body.title is concatenated directly into the SQL query string without sanitization, allowing SQL injection.
findings.mitigation: Use a parameterized query with placeholders and bound parameters for the title value.

findings.title: Missing input validation
findings.attack_vector: There is no validation on the input title, allowing potential storage of non-compliant data.
findings.mitigation: Implement input validation to ensure title meets expected format and constraints (e.g., type, length).

findings.title: SQL injection
findings.attack_vector: req.params.id is concatenated directly into the SQL query, allowing for potential SQL injection.
findings.mitigation: Use parameterized queries by passing id as a parameter in the db.get function.

findings.title: SQL injection
findings.attack_vector: req.body.title is concatenated directly into the SQL query, allowing for potential SQL injection.
findings.mitigation: Use parameterized queries by passing title as a parameter in the db.run function.

findings.title: Missing input validation
findings.attack_vector: No validation on req.body.title, potentially leading to unexpected behavior or data.
findings.mitigation: Implement input validation to ensure title meets expected format and constraints.

findings.title: SQL injection
findings.attack_vector: req.params.id is concatenated directly into the query, allowing an attacker to manipulate the SQL query, e.g., /todos/1 OR 1=1 --
findings.mitigation: Use parameterized queries by refactoring the query to 'SELECT * FROM todos WHERE id = ?', [id].

findings.title: SQL injection
findings.attack_vector: req.body.title is directly interpolated into the SQL string, allowing an attacker to manipulate the SQL query, e.g., sending title="test'); DROP TABLE todos;--"
findings.mitigation: Use parameterized queries by refactoring the query to 'INSERT INTO todos (title) VALUES (?)', [title].

findings.title: Missing input validation
findings.attack_vector: The API does not validate the input 'title', leading to potential injection or unexpected data issues.
findings.mitigation: Validate the input 'title' for expected format, length, and character set to ensure it adheres to business logic requirements.

findings.title: SQL injection
findings.attack_vector: Untrusted input from req.params.id is concatenated directly into the SQL query in getTodoById, allowing SQL injection via 'req.params.id = 1 OR 1=1'.
findings.mitigation: Use a parameterized query: db.get('SELECT * FROM todos WHERE id = ?', [id], ...).

findings.title: SQL injection
findings.attack_vector: Untrusted input from req.body.title is concatenated directly into the SQL query in createTodo, allowing SQL injection if title contains SQL statements.
findings.mitigation: Use a parameterized query: db.run('INSERT INTO todos (title) VALUES (?)', [title], ...).

findings.title: Missing input validation
findings.attack_vector: User-provided inputs (id and title) are not validated or sanitized, potentially allowing unexpected data to be processed.
findings.mitigation: Implement input validation for req.params.id and req.body.title to ensure they match expected types and constraints.

findings.title: Missing input validation
findings.attack_vector: Client can send requests with invalid titles or completed status without validation, which could lead to data inconsistency or corruption.
findings.mitigation: Implement input validation by checking the type and length of `title`, and ensure `completed` is a boolean before processing the request.

findings.title: Missing input validation
findings.attack_vector: User inputs for creating and updating a todo title are not validated, potentially allowing excessively long or malicious input.
findings.mitigation: Implement input validation to ensure 'title' is within acceptable length and format, preventing potential misuse or strain on resources.

findings.title: Lack of authorization checks
findings.attack_vector: Any user can update or delete any todo item due to missing ownership checks.
findings.mitigation: Implement authorization logic to ensure users can only modify their own todo items, enforcing proper access control.

findings.title: missing input validation
findings.attack_vector: Unvalidated inputs `title`, `completed` in create/update operations could lead to malformed or unwanted data persistence.
findings.mitigation: Add input validation for `title` and `completed` ensuring they meet expected formats and constraints before database interactions.

findings.title: Missing input validation
findings.attack_vector: Unvalidated input in request body and parameters; potential data integrity issues.
findings.mitigation: Implement input validation for title, completed, and id fields to enforce type and length constraints.

findings.title: missing input validation
findings.attack_vector: The input 'title' and 'completed' from req.body can be arbitrary data, potentially causing unexpected behavior or application errors.
findings.mitigation: Implement input validation to ensure 'title' is a non-empty string and 'completed' is a boolean and within expected values before database operations are executed.

findings.title: Missing input validation
findings.attack_vector: Unvalidated input in POST and PUT requests could lead to unexpected behavior if malicious data is passed.
findings.mitigation: Implement input validation to ensure `title` is a non-empty string and `completed` is a boolean in the `createTodo` and `updateTodo` functions.

findings.title: Missing input validation
findings.attack_vector: Title of the todo item from req.body can include unexpected data type or length.
findings.mitigation: Add validation on the 'title' field to ensure it is of the correct type and length before using it in database operations.

findings.title: Potential denial of service due to unbounded resource usage
findings.attack_vector: The current implementation retrieves all todos without any limit or pagination, which can lead to high memory usage and eventual denial of service for large data sets.
findings.mitigation: Implement pagination on the GET /todos endpoint to limit the number of todos returned in a single response, mitigating memory usage concerns.

findings.title: Missing input validation
findings.attack_vector: Unrestricted input in 'title' and 'completed' fields allowing potentially harmful input through.
findings.mitigation: Implement input validation to check the type, length, and acceptable values for 'title' and 'completed' fields before database insertion.

findings.title: World-readable database file
findings.attack_vector: Database file 'todos.db' could be world-readable and writable, leading to unauthorized data access or modification.
findings.mitigation: Change the file permissions of 'todos.db' to restrict access only to the application user.

findings.title: missing input validation
findings.attack_vector: An attacker could send a malformed or overly large title in the POST /todos endpoint, potentially causing unexpected behavior.
findings.mitigation: Implement input validation for the 'title' field in 'createTodo' and 'updateTodo' to ensure it meets expected formats and length.

findings.title: missing input validation
findings.attack_vector: An attacker could send a non-integer 'completed' value in the PUT /todos/:id endpoint, leading to incorrect database values.
findings.mitigation: Implement input validation to ensure the 'completed' field is a valid integer (0 or 1) in the 'updateTodo' function.

findings.title: missing input validation
findings.attack_vector: User could supply excessively long or otherwise unexpected strings in 'title', potentially leading to unexpected behavior or application errors.
findings.mitigation: Implement validation for 'title' in the createTodo and updateTodo functions to ensure it meets expected format and length constraints.

findings.title: Missing input validation
findings.attack_vector: Client can send arbitrary JSON data without validation for title or completed fields.
findings.mitigation: Implement input validation for request body fields (e.g., title and completed) using a validation library or manual checks.

findings.title: Missing Input Validation
findings.attack_vector: An attacker could send a request with a very large title or potentially harmful string that could cause issues
findings.mitigation: Implement input validation on 'title' to ensure it meets expected length and character requirements

findings.title: Missing Input Validation
findings.attack_vector: An attacker could send requests with malformed 'title' or 'completed' values causing unexpected behavior
findings.mitigation: Validate 'title' and ensure 'completed' is a boolean value before processing the update

findings.title: Missing input validation
findings.attack_vector: User can submit a todo with an empty or excessively long title, potentially leading to unexpected application behavior or database issues.
findings.mitigation: Add input validation to check that `title` is a non-empty string and ensure it does not exceed a reasonable length limit.

findings.title: Insecure data initialization
findings.attack_vector: The SQLite database is created in the parent directory, which might not have strict permissions, leading to potential unauthorized access or modification of the database file.
findings.mitigation: Ensure the database file is stored in a directory with restricted permissions, limiting access to the application user only.

notes: security_v6 = security_v5 (few-shot) VERBATIM + ONE threat_level/severity disambiguation note (no DF-fixture tokens).
PRIMARY metric is INVALID_ROLE_OUTPUT across all 18 trials (TARGET 0). v5 had 2/14 (DF-2 trial-2, DF-3 trial-1).
DF-2 recall = SQLi BLOCKER + threat HIGH/CRITICAL on EVERY PARSED trial. DF-3 precision = no SQLi-FP AND no BLOCKER leak on every parsed trial.
DF-4 over-fire PASS = no-over-fire ≥7/8 (BLOCKER + SQLi-FP both absent). input-val/auth BLOCKER tracked.
reviewer untouched (reviewer_v5 8/8 in STEP E — not re-run). Model overridden per-call (gpt-4o); .env untouched.
security_clean = (INVALID==0) AND (over-fire ≥7/8) AND recall holds AND precision holds.
Raw per-trial outputs under raw/ are the ground truth. NO CLOSURE — CTO ratifies.
